codeReview.summary: Solid implementation that fully covers the feature requirements. All 7 new files (types.ts, constants.ts, invoices.ts, payment.ts, index.ts, useInvoices.ts, and test file) are well-structured and follow project conventions. Invoice CRUD is backed by Supabase with proper error handling. Settlement wrapper correctly integrates thirdweb x402 settlePayment/facilitator with Arbitrum USDC. Server-side-only documentation in payment.ts is clear and appropriate for a Vite SPA. 34 tests cover constants, types, CRUD operations, settlement logic, React Query hooks, and index re-exports. Only minor non-blocking issues found.

issues.description: Fallthrough to 402 for all non-200 statuses: the else branch after the status===200 check unconditionally returns a 402 PaymentResult. If thirdweb settlePayment returns an unexpected status (e.g. 500 server error, 403 forbidden), the caller would see a misleading '402 Payment required' message instead of the actual error. Consider checking for result.status !== 402 and returning a generic error with the actual status code.

txHash extraction uses double type assertion: `(result.paymentReceipt as Record<string, unknown>).txHash as string ?? ""`. The `as string` assertion before `??` is technically safe at runtime (undefined ?? '' still works), but could mask type issues. A safer pattern: `String((result.paymentReceipt as Record<string, unknown>)?.txHash ?? '')`.

useCreateInvoice and useUpdateInvoicePayment mutation hooks are exported but have no test coverage. Only the query hooks (useInvoice, useInvoices) are tested. While the mutations are thin wrappers, testing that cache invalidation works correctly (invalidateQueries on success) would increase confidence.

USDC address was split into USDC_ADDR_SUFFIX + template literal concatenation to work around Droid-Shield false positive detecting it as a secret. The address is a well-known public contract on Arbitrum. The workaround is functional but adds unnecessary indirection.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker had to deal with Droid-Shield blocking git commits due to the USDC contract address being flagged as a potential secret. The worker's workaround (splitting the address into a suffix constant + template literal) is functional but introduces unnecessary code complexity. Public blockchain contract addresses are not secrets — this pattern should be documented so future workers don't waste time on the same false positive.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows 3 failed commit attempts before the worker restructured constants.ts to split the address. The address 0xaf88d065e77c8cC2239327C5EDb3A432268e5831 is the canonical USDC contract on Arbitrum One.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed the synthesis-worker procedure correctly: read reference sources first, wrote tests (TDD), implemented, verified with npm test / tsc / build. skillFeedback.followedProcedure is true with no deviations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows reference file reads before implementation, test file created before or alongside implementation files, all 3 verification commands run successfully.

summary: PASS. The x402 payment infrastructure feature is fully implemented with 7 new files across src/lib/x402/, src/hooks/, and src/test/. All feature requirements are met: constants.ts has correct USDC address and chain config, invoices.ts provides full CRUD backed by Supabase, payment.ts wraps thirdweb x402 settlePayment with proper server-side-only documentation, types.ts defines Invoice/LineItem/PaymentResult, and useInvoices.ts provides React Query hooks. 34 tests pass, typecheck is clean, and build succeeds. Only non-blocking issues found: the settlement error fallthrough to 402 for all non-200 statuses, mutation hooks lacking test coverage, and the USDC address splitting workaround for Droid-Shield.
